dasabalassa sƒÅsana·πÅ.goenka: Dasabala, "the one of ten powers", is an epithet of the TathƒÅgata. The ten powers are ten fields of perfect knowledge: he understands according to reality (yathƒÅbh≈´ta·πÅ pajƒÅnƒÅti) what is possible and what is impossible; the results of past, present and future actions with regard to their bases and causes; where any path of practice leads ‚Äî to the hell realm, animal realm, ghost realm, human realm, deva realm, or to nibbƒÅna; the world with its numerous and diverse elements; the diverse dispositions of beings; the range of faculties of other beings and persons; the defilement and purification associated with the jhƒÅnas, liberations, concentrations and attainments; and the remaining powers of recollecting past lives, the divine eye, and the destruction of the taints. The phrase dasabalassa sƒÅsana·πÅ thus means the dispensation of the Buddha so endowed.

They dwell above the human realm, beginning with the realm of the four great kings (cƒÅtumahƒÅrƒÅjikƒÅ devƒÅ), the sphere of the thirty-three (tƒÅvati·πÅsƒÅ devƒÅ), the field of the yƒÅmƒÅ devƒÅ, the tusitƒÅ devƒÅ, those who delight in creation (nimmƒÅnaratƒ´ devƒÅ), and those dwelling in the sphere of beings with power over the creations of others (paranimmitavasavattƒ´ devƒÅ).

dhamma.meaning: Dhamma: the teaching of the Buddha; the universal law of nature; the path. dhamma: phenomenon; mental object; quality; thing
dhamma.summary: Capitalised Dhamma and lowercase dhamma are distinguished senses of the same word.
breakdown: Root meaning to hold, uphold, support: that which upholds.
dhamma.goenka: Capitalisation carries the distinction. Dhamma (capitalised) denotes the teaching of the Buddha, the universal law of nature, and the path itself; dhamma (lowercase) denotes a phenomenon, a mental object, an element of experience. Klaus uses Dhamma capitalised throughout to denote both the Buddha's teaching and the universal law. He explicitly distinguishes saddhamma, the true Dhamma, which contains seven qualities: saddhƒÅ (faith), sati (awareness), hirƒ´ (sense of shame), ottappa (moral sense of guilt), bahusacca (ample learning), vƒ´riya (diligence) and pa√±√±ƒÅ (wisdom). The phrase saddhammassavana·πÅ, listening to the true Dhamma, is presented as one of the rarities (dullabho) ‚Äî encountering the teaching is itself rare. Volume 2's title, SvƒÅkkhƒÅto ca Parisuddho Dhammo, means "So Well-Explained and Utterly Pure Dhamma". The PƒÅli term is retained in tradition usage; it is not replaced by the Sanskrit dharma.
